title: Distributed Cache
prompt: Design a distributed caching system like Memcached or Redis Cluster. The system should provide low-latency key-value lookups, support LRU eviction, use consistent hashing for data distribution, and handle replication for fault tolerance.

functional: LRU eviction when memory limit is reached
Consistent hashing for distributing keys across nodes
Replication for fault tolerance (configurable replication factor)
nonFunctional: High availability (99.999%) — cache downtime causes cascading failures
Graceful handling of node failures without data loss for replicated keys

scale.storage: 100 TB total cached data across 1000 nodes, each with 128 GB RAM

keyQuestions: What consistency model is required (strong vs eventual)?
What is the expected read:write ratio?
Do we need persistence or is this purely in-memory?
What is the acceptable cache miss rate?
How should the system behave during node failures (serve stale vs miss)?
Do we need support for complex data structures (lists, sets, sorted sets)?

keyCalculations: Total ops: 100M ops/sec across 1000 nodes = 100K ops/sec per node
Memory per node: 128 GB RAM, ~100 GB usable for cache data
Average value size: 1 KB => 100M keys per node, 100B keys total
Network bandwidth per node: 100K ops * 1 KB = 100 MB/sec (within 10 Gbps NIC)
Replication overhead: RF=3 means 3x write amplification, 300M writes/sec cluster-wide for 100M client writes
Hash ring: 1000 physical nodes * 100 virtual nodes = 100K points on ring

expectedEndpoints: CAS(key, expectedValue, newValue) => OK or CONFLICT
MGET(keys[]) => { results[] } (batch get for reduced round trips)

expectedEntities: cache_entry: key (bytes), value (bytes), ttl_expiry_timestamp, cas_version, created_at, last_accessed_at
node_metadata: node_id, host, port, status (active/draining/dead), memory_used, memory_total
eviction_queue: doubly-linked list per node for LRU ordering

expectedComponents: Client Library (consistent hash routing, connection pooling, retry logic)
Cache Node (in-memory hash table + LRU eviction)
Consistent Hash Ring (virtual nodes for balanced distribution)
Replication Manager (sync/async replication to replica nodes)
Cluster Coordinator (node membership, failure detection via gossip/heartbeat)
Hot Key Detector (identify and mitigate hot keys)

suggestedTopics: Consistent hashing: virtual nodes, bounded load balancing, handling node addition/removal with minimal redistribution
LRU eviction: exact LRU (doubly-linked list + hash map) vs approximated LRU (sampling) for performance
Replication: sync vs async, quorum reads/writes (R + W > N), conflict resolution for eventual consistency
Hot key mitigation: client-side caching, key splitting/sharding, local read replicas
Failure handling: detecting node failure (phi accrual failure detector), re-replication, consistent hashing keyspace takeover
Memory management: slab allocation, avoiding fragmentation, jemalloc vs tcmalloc

checklist: Asked about failure behavior (stale vs miss)

anchors.1: Skipped requirements, started designing without understanding consistency or scale needs
anchors.2: Asked about data size but missed consistency model or failure behavior
anchors.3: Covered consistency, scale, eviction policy, and failure handling requirements
anchors.4: Proactively identified hot key risks, cache stampede scenarios, replication consistency trade-offs, and client-side caching needs

checklist: Used consistent hashing for key distribution
Addressed client-side routing and connection management

anchors.1: Single cache server with no distribution, replication, or eviction strategy
anchors.2: Multiple cache nodes with simple hash partitioning but no consistent hashing or replication
anchors.3: Consistent hashing with virtual nodes, replication, LRU eviction, and cluster coordination
anchors.4: Smart client routing, bounded-load consistent hashing, quorum-based replication, hot key detection, and graceful node draining

checklist: Explained consistent hashing with virtual nodes
Addressed replication consistency (quorum, sync vs async)
Considered hot key detection and mitigation
Discussed node failure detection and recovery

anchors.1: No depth on consistent hashing, eviction, or replication mechanisms
anchors.2: Basic consistent hashing explanation but no discussion of virtual nodes, eviction, or replication
anchors.3: Deep dive into 2+ areas: consistent hashing + replication or eviction with concrete algorithms
anchors.4: Thorough analysis covering virtual node balancing, approximated LRU performance, quorum tuning, phi accrual failure detection, and memory management

anchors.1: Could not identify any trade-offs in the design
anchors.3: Discussed 2-3 trade-offs with pros/cons covering replication, eviction, or consistency
anchors.4: Systematically evaluated consistency, performance, and availability trade-offs with quantitative analysis of latency impact

commonMistakes: Using simple modular hashing instead of consistent hashing, causing massive redistribution on node changes
Ignoring hot keys that concentrate load on a single node
Not considering cache stampede (thundering herd) when a popular key expires
Choosing synchronous replication without understanding the latency cost
Implementing exact LRU with global locks, destroying throughput
Not addressing split-brain scenarios during network partitions
